claims.claim_text: The opening January 2024 gaming-company expansion story presents a real bare-metal Kubernetes event with specific fleet size, hardware models, and procurement outcome.
claims.anchor_text: In January 2024, a gaming company running a 120-node bare metal Kubernetes cluster needed to add 40 new servers for a game launch. Their existing cluster used Intel Xeon Silver 4214 (Cascade Lake, 2019) processors. The procurement team ordered Dell R760 servers with AMD EPYC 9354 (Genoa, 2023) processors because they offered better price/performance. The infrastructure team racked the servers, PXE-booted them, and joined them to the cluster. Everything appeared fine until the scheduler started placing workloads on the new nodes.
claims.suggested_rewrite: When a team expands a large bare-metal Kubernetes cluster with a newer hardware generation, scheduling behavior can change in ways that are not obvious during initial bring-up.
claims.rationale: This is an unattributed war story with named timing, hardware, and business context.

claims.claim_text: The module presents exact workload performance deltas and team behavior from the same unnamed migration incident.
claims.anchor_text: The Java applications ran 15% faster on the AMD nodes due to higher single-thread performance. The data science team's TensorFlow jobs ran 40% faster because the EPYC CPUs had AVX-512 support. But here was the problem: the Kubernetes scheduler does not understand CPU performance differences. It sees "48 CPU cores available" on both the old Intel and new AMD nodes. The scheduler distributed pods evenly, but the AMD nodes could handle more work per core. Some teams manually pinned their pods to AMD nodes using nodeSelectors, creating an uneven resource distribution. Other teams complained that their pods were "slow" when scheduled on the older Intel nodes.
claims.suggested_rewrite: Workloads can perform differently across CPU generations, while the default Kubernetes scheduler still reasons about requested CPU as a quantity rather than benchmarked per-core performance. If teams respond by manually pinning workloads to newer nodes, overall cluster utilization can become uneven.
claims.rationale: This bundles precise benchmark-like deltas into an unsourced anecdote.

claims.claim_text: The module presents a precise decommissioning failure from the same unnamed incident, including pod counts and utilization figures.
claims.anchor_text: The real disaster came when the team tried to decommission 20 of the oldest Intel servers. They had not configured topology spread constraints, so draining those nodes pushed 300 pods onto nodes that were already at 75% utilization. The cluster hit 95% CPU utilization, OOM kills spiked, and the monitoring stack (also on the cluster) slowed to a crawl -- right when they needed it most.
claims.suggested_rewrite: Decommissioning older nodes without spread constraints and spare capacity can overload the remaining cluster, trigger evictions or OOM-related instability, and even degrade the monitoring systems you need during the change.
claims.rationale: This is an incident-style claim with precise operational numbers but no attributable source.

claims.claim_text: In Kubernetes 1.35, cgroup v1 is deprecated and kubelet no longer starts on cgroup v1 nodes by default.
claims.rationale: The upstream cgroups documentation explicitly documents the v1.35 deprecation and default kubelet behavior.

claims.claim_text: With cgroup v2, kubelet and the container runtime should use the same cgroup driver, and systemd is the recommended driver on systemd-based Linux nodes.
claims.rationale: The container runtime documentation covers the shared-driver requirement and the cgroup v2/systemd recommendation.

claims.claim_text: Vanilla Cluster Autoscaler does not handle on-prem bare-metal node provisioning, so bare-metal expansion requires separate provisioning automation.
claims.rationale: Upstream Cluster Autoscaler docs assume node groups and external provisioning/registration tooling, but they do not state this bare-metal limitation in exactly those words.

claims.claim_text: kubeadm installations require unique hostnames, MAC addresses, and product_uuid values for each node or installation can fail.
claims.rationale: The kubeadm install guide explicitly requires these identifiers to be unique and warns that installation may fail otherwise.

claims.claim_text: In Kubernetes 1.35, the Topology Manager `max-allowable-numa-nodes` option is GA.
claims.rationale: The Topology Manager task page states that `max-allowable-numa-nodes` is GA in Kubernetes 1.35.

claims.claim_text: The Xeon Silver 4214 table row gives exact year, core count, single-thread score, and PassMark score.
claims.rationale: This looks real and benchmark-backed, but the natural primary/benchmark source is off the current allowlist.

claims.claim_text: The Xeon Gold 6330 table row gives exact year, core count, single-thread score, and PassMark score.
claims.rationale: This appears verifiable from an off-allowlist benchmark source.

claims.claim_text: The EPYC 9354 table row gives exact year, core count, single-thread score, and PassMark score.
claims.rationale: This appears verifiable from an off-allowlist benchmark source.

claims.claim_text: The module states that EPYC 9354 delivers about 44% more single-thread and 3.5x more multi-thread performance than Xeon Silver 4214.
claims.anchor_text: The EPYC 9354 delivers ~44% more single-thread and ~3.5x more multi-thread performance than the 4214.
claims.suggested_rewrite: The EPYC 9354 substantially outperforms the 4214 in both single-threaded and multithreaded benchmark data, but the exact percentage depends on the benchmark source and snapshot date.
claims.rationale: The exact percentages are benchmark-snapshot dependent and do not cleanly match current live benchmark figures.

claims.claim_text: The weighted-capacity example states that removing 20 older Xeon 4214 nodes from a 120-node mixed cluster removes only about 8% of effective compute capacity rather than 17%.
claims.anchor_text: Kubernetes sees all CPU cores as equal, but they are not. Use benchmark scores (e.g., Passmark single-thread) to calculate "normalized" capacity. Example: decommissioning 20 older Xeon 4214 nodes from a 120-node mixed cluster removes only ~8% of effective compute capacity, not the 17% that a simple node count would suggest. Always use weighted capacity calculations when planning decommissions.
claims.suggested_rewrite: Kubernetes sees all CPU cores as equal, but they are not. Use benchmark data to calculate normalized capacity, because removing older nodes can reduce effective compute by much less than raw node counts suggest. Always use weighted capacity calculations when planning decommissions.
claims.rationale: This precise example depends on unstated fleet composition and benchmark assumptions.

claims.claim_text: Local PersistentVolumes are tied to specific nodes via node affinity and can become inaccessible or lost when the node is removed.
claims.rationale: The volumes documentation explains local PV node affinity and the reduced availability/data-loss risk tied to the underlying node.

claims.claim_text: Graceful Node Shutdown is enabled by default in Kubernetes, but the shutdown behavior is not activated unless `shutdownGracePeriod` and related settings are configured to non-zero values.
claims.rationale: The upstream node shutdown docs explicitly describe the default-enabled gate and the zero-value configuration caveat.

claims.claim_text: The refresh-cycle section introduces a 100-node, $10,000-per-node cost model as if it were a factual baseline.
claims.suggested_rewrite: For a 100-node cluster, refresh-cycle cost depends heavily on hardware pricing, support terms, energy costs, and workload requirements:
claims.rationale: This is a scenario setup with a precise price point but no attributable model or source.

claims.claim_text: The table states exact annualized CapEx values for 3-year and 5-year refresh cycles.
claims.suggested_rewrite: | Amortized CapEx/year | Higher annualized spend with faster refresh | Lower annualized spend with slower refresh |
claims.rationale: These exact figures are illustrative model outputs rather than cited published data.

claims.claim_text: The table states exact total support-contract costs for 3-year and 5-year cycles.
claims.anchor_text: | Support contracts | $150,000 total | $310,000 total (higher in years 4-5) |
claims.suggested_rewrite: | Support contracts | Typically lower over a shorter lifecycle | Typically higher as hardware ages |
claims.rationale: Support pricing varies widely by vendor, contract, and hardware generation.

claims.claim_text: The table states exact total power costs and a 5% yearly efficiency degradation assumption.
claims.suggested_rewrite: | Power (total) | Typically lower with newer, more efficient nodes | Typically higher when older nodes stay in service longer |
claims.rationale: Both the dollar totals and the degradation rate are uncited model assumptions.

claims.claim_text: The table states exact end-of-life failure-rate bands for 3-year and 5-year cycles.
claims.suggested_rewrite: | Failure rate (end of life) | Typically lower | Typically higher |
claims.rationale: These exact failure bands are presented without a source and depend heavily on fleet composition and operating conditions.

claims.claim_text: The table states exact performance-retention values versus current generation hardware.
claims.anchor_text: | Performance vs current gen | 100% at refresh | 60% at end of life |
claims.suggested_rewrite: | Performance vs current gen | Closer to current-generation performance | Further behind current-generation performance |
claims.rationale: These exact percentages are uncited and depend on workload and benchmark selection.

claims.claim_text: The table states exact total-cost totals for the 3-year and 5-year scenarios.
claims.suggested_rewrite: | **Total cost** | **Depends on your hardware, power, support, and failure assumptions** | **Depends on your hardware, power, support, and failure assumptions** |
claims.rationale: These are model outputs built on uncited assumptions rather than attributable published totals.

claims.claim_text: The module concludes that the 3-year cycle is exactly 24% more expensive per year.
claims.anchor_text: The 3-year cycle is 24% more expensive per year but keeps you on the latest hardware with lower failure rates and power costs.
claims.suggested_rewrite: A shorter refresh cycle usually increases annualized capital spend, while a longer cycle can increase operational risk, support burden, and power costs.
claims.rationale: The exact percentage depends on the uncited scenario assumptions used in the table.

claims.claim_text: Kubernetes v1.35 large-cluster tested limits are 5,000 nodes, 110 pods per node, 150,000 total pods, and 300,000 total containers.
claims.rationale: These exact scalability limits are documented in the upstream large-cluster guidance.

claims.claim_text: The containerd compatibility bullet says Kubernetes 1.35 is the last release to support containerd 1.7 and that fallback compatibility is completely removed in Kubernetes 1.36.
claims.anchor_text: - **Kubernetes 1.35 is the last release to support containerd 1.7.** If your hardware refresh involves reinstalling the operating system and container runtime on new servers, ensure you install containerd 2.0+ (or another CRI v1 compliant runtime). The fallback compatibility for older runtimes will be completely removed in Kubernetes 1.36.
claims.suggested_rewrite: - **Kubernetes 1.35 is the last release to support the containerd 1.x series.** If your hardware refresh involves reinstalling the operating system and container runtime on new servers, plan to use containerd 2.x or another CRI-conformant runtime before upgrading beyond 1.35, because newer kubelets continue tightening runtime compatibility.
claims.rationale: The 1.35 release note supports the last-support point, but current upstream docs say the older-runtime fallback is dropped later than 1.36.

claims.claim_text: The Intel/AMD socket bullet presents an absolute hardware-compatibility claim without product-specific sourcing.
claims.anchor_text: - **Intel and AMD use different socket standards, so you cannot swap CPUs between vendors in the same server chassis.** A hardware refresh that switches from Intel to AMD (or vice versa) requires entirely new servers, not just new CPUs. This is why vendor choice in the initial purchase has long-term implications.
claims.suggested_rewrite: - **Switching CPU vendors usually means replacing the server platform, not just the processor, because server sockets and platform compatibility differ by vendor and generation.** This is why vendor choice in the initial purchase has long-term implications.
claims.rationale: The operational lesson is sound, but the absolute wording needs product-specific platform-compatibility sources.

claims.claim_text: The DOE supercomputing bullet presents a named institutional refresh policy with exact planning and overlap durations.
claims.anchor_text: - **The US Department of Energy's supercomputing centers use a 5-year refresh cycle** because their systems cost hundreds of millions of dollars. They plan each refresh 3 years in advance, with a 2-year overlap period where old and new systems run simultaneously. This staggered approach is now being adopted by large Kubernetes operators.
claims.suggested_rewrite: - **Large HPC operators often plan refreshes years in advance and may run old and new systems in parallel during transitions.** Similar overlap planning can help large Kubernetes operators reduce migration risk during hardware refreshes.
claims.rationale: This is a named real-world operations claim with specific timing but no attributable source.

claims.claim_text: The In-place Pod Resize bullet combines a correct GA status claim with an overstated 'without restarting them' behavior claim.
claims.anchor_text: - **Kubernetes 1.35 graduated In-place Pod Resize to General Availability (GA).** This means you can resize the CPU and memory requests of running containers without restarting them. This is incredibly useful when migrating long-running workloads between hardware generations that possess different baseline performance characteristics, as you can dynamically adjust resources as the pod lands on a faster or slower node.
claims.suggested_rewrite: - **Kubernetes 1.35 graduated In-place Pod Resize to General Availability (GA).** This lets you change CPU and memory requests and limits for running containers without recreating the Pod, which can reduce disruption when migrating long-running workloads across mixed hardware.
claims.rationale: The GA status is citable, but the 'without restarting them' wording overstates current upstream guarantees.

claims.claim_text: Kubernetes 1.24 added the `MinDomainsInPodTopologySpread` feature and it was stable in 1.30.
claims.rationale: The topology spread documentation states the pre-1.30 gate requirement and the stable availability from 1.30 onward.

claims.claim_text: The Uptime Institute bullet combines a specific 2023 report statistic with a precise two-year payback generalization.
claims.anchor_text: - **A 2023 Uptime Institute report found that the average server lifespan has increased from 3.5 years in 2015 to 5.2 years in 2023**, driven by companies extending server lifecycles to reduce CapEx during economic uncertainty. However, power efficiency improvements mean newer servers often pay for themselves in energy savings within 2 years.
claims.suggested_rewrite: - **Recent industry reporting suggests that many operators are extending server lifecycles beyond the traditional three-year window.** Even so, newer hardware can still offer meaningful efficiency gains, so refresh timing should be based on measured total cost of ownership rather than purchase price alone.
claims.rationale: This combines an off-allowlist report claim with an uncited payback assertion.

further_reading.title: Pod Topology Spread Constraints
further_reading.why_relevant: Covers `maxSkew`, `whenUnsatisfiable`, `matchLabelKeys`, and `minDomains` for rack-aware and hardware-aware spreading.

further_reading.title: Node Shutdowns
further_reading.why_relevant: Explains graceful node shutdown, `shutdownGracePeriod` settings, and the node-state behavior relevant to decommissioning.

further_reading.title: Considerations for Large Clusters
further_reading.why_relevant: Provides the official tested scalability limits that matter for long-term capacity planning.

further_reading.title: Control Topology Management Policies on a node
further_reading.why_relevant: Details Topology Manager options, including `max-allowable-numa-nodes`, for modern multi-socket systems.

notes: No shared citation pool exists for this section, so `section_pool_ref` is null and `source_ids` remain empty. Quiz content, exercise content, diagrams, and code blocks were excluded per pipeline rules; factual table rows and Did You Know bullets were included where they made sourceable or unsourceable factual claims.
